instruction: Implement a fan-out/fan-in pipeline in Go that distributes work across N workers and merges results into a single channel.
Implement the or-channel pattern in Go: a variadic function that returns a channel that closes when ANY of the input channels closes.
output:
<think>
The or-channel is a classic Dave Cheney pattern. The naive approach is to select across all inputs, but select requires a fixed number of cases at compile time. The recursive solution picks the first two channels, selects between them and a recursive call on the rest. This runs in O(n log n) goroutines — acceptable for small n.

Edge cases: zero inputs → return nil channel (blocks forever). One input → return it directly.
</think>

```go
package main

import "fmt"

// or returns a channel that closes when any input channel closes or sends.
func or(channels ...<-chan struct{}) <-chan struct{} {
	switch len(channels) {
	case 0:
		return nil
	case 1:
		return channels[0]
	}

	orDone := make(chan struct{})
	go func() {
		defer close(orDone)
		switch len(channels) {
		case 2:
			select {
			case <-channels[0]:
			case <-channels[1]:
			}
		default:
			select {
			case <-channels[0]:
			case <-channels[1]:
			case <-channels[2]:
			case <-or(append(channels[3:], orDone)...):
			}
		}
	}()
	return orDone
}

func sig(after string) <-chan struct{} {
	ch := make(chan struct{})
	go func() {
		defer close(ch)
		fmt.Println("closing after", after)
	}()
	return ch
}

func main() {
	done := or(sig("a"), sig("b"), sig("c"))
	<-done
	fmt.Println("one of the channels closed")
}
```

The recursion feeds `orDone` back into the tail call so that when the top-level goroutine exits, the entire tree of goroutines unblocks and cleans up — no leaks.

instruction: Implement a tee-channel in Go that duplicates every value from one input channel into two output channels.
Build a bridge-channel in Go: a function that flattens a channel-of-channels into a single channel of values.
Implement token-bucket rate limiting in Go using time.Ticker without any third-party libraries.
output:
<think>
A token bucket refills at a fixed rate. We can model it with a buffered channel as the bucket and a ticker as the refill mechanism. Cap the bucket at burst size. Each request drains one token; if the bucket is empty the caller blocks until a token arrives.
</think>

```go
package main

import (
	"context"
	"fmt"
	"time"
)

// RateLimiter is a token-bucket rate limiter backed by a channel.
type RateLimiter struct {
	tokens chan struct{}
	ticker *time.Ticker
	cancel context.CancelFunc
}

// NewRateLimiter creates a limiter that allows `rate` events/sec with burst `burst`.
func NewRateLimiter(ctx context.Context, rate int, burst int) *RateLimiter {
	tokens := make(chan struct{}, burst)
	// Pre-fill bucket.
	for i := 0; i < burst; i++ {
		tokens <- struct{}{}
	}

	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(ctx)
	ticker := time.NewTicker(time.Second / time.Duration(rate))

	go func() {
		defer ticker.Stop()
		for {
			select {
			case <-ticker.C:
				select {
				case tokens <- struct{}{}: // refill one token
				default: // bucket full, drop
				}
			case <-ctx.Done():
				return
			}
		}
	}()

	return &RateLimiter{tokens: tokens, ticker: ticker, cancel: cancel}
}

// Wait blocks until a token is available or ctx is cancelled.
func (rl *RateLimiter) Wait(ctx context.Context) error {
	select {
	case <-rl.tokens:
		return nil
	case <-ctx.Done():
		return ctx.Err()
	}
}

// Stop shuts down the background refill goroutine.
func (rl *RateLimiter) Stop() { rl.cancel() }

func main() {
	ctx := context.Background()
	limiter := NewRateLimiter(ctx, 5, 3) // 5 RPS, burst 3
	defer limiter.Stop()

	for i := 0; i < 10; i++ {
		if err := limiter.Wait(ctx); err != nil {
			fmt.Println("cancelled:", err)
			return
		}
		fmt.Printf("request %d at %v\n", i, time.Now().Format("15:04:05.000"))
	}
}
```

The inner `select { default: }` on the refill send is critical: it makes the token deposit non-blocking so the ticker goroutine never stalls if the bucket is already full.

instruction: Explain and demonstrate the difference between buffered and unbuffered channels in Go with a concrete synchronization example.
Implement a pipeline pattern in Go with three stages: generate integers, square them, and filter evens — each stage in its own goroutine.
Implement a channel-based work queue with a fixed-size worker pool in Go, processing jobs and collecting results.
output:
<think>
Classic worker pool: one jobs channel, one results channel, N goroutines. Close jobs when all work is submitted. Collect results with a separate goroutine that waits on a WaitGroup then closes results.
</think>

```go
package main

import (
	"fmt"
	"math/rand"
	"sync"
	"time"
)

type Job struct {
	ID    int
	Value int
}

type Result struct {
	Job    Job
	Output int
}

func worker(id int, jobs <-chan Job, results chan<- Result, wg *sync.WaitGroup) {
	defer wg.Done()
	for job := range jobs {
		// Simulate variable work duration.
		time.Sleep(time.Duration(rand.Intn(100)) * time.Millisecond)
		results <- Result{Job: job, Output: job.Value * job.Value}
		fmt.Printf("worker %d processed job %d\n", id, job.ID)
	}
}

func main() {
	const numWorkers = 4
	const numJobs = 20

	jobs := make(chan Job, numJobs)
	results := make(chan Result, numJobs)

	var wg sync.WaitGroup
	for w := 1; w <= numWorkers; w++ {
		wg.Add(1)
		go worker(w, jobs, results, &wg)
	}

	// Submit jobs.
	for j := 1; j <= numJobs; j++ {
		jobs <- Job{ID: j, Value: j}
	}
	close(jobs) // signal workers: no more jobs

	// Close results once all workers are done.
	go func() {
		wg.Wait()
		close(results)
	}()

	// Collect results.
	var total int
	for r := range results {
		total += r.Output
	}
	fmt.Println("total:", total)
}
```

Closing `jobs` is the clean shutdown signal — workers drain remaining items then exit, decrementing the WaitGroup. The anonymous goroutine that calls `wg.Wait()` then `close(results)` is the idiomatic way to signal the collector that all results are in.

instruction: Demonstrate the repeat and take pipeline generators in Go — infinite source + finite consumer connected via channels.
Show how to implement a bounded semaphore in Go using a buffered channel and use it to limit concurrent HTTP requests.
Implement a context-aware pipeline stage that drains an input channel respecting cancellation, known as the orDone pattern.
Implement a sliding window rate limiter using errgroup and context in Go to process tasks with bounded concurrency and collect errors.
Build a worker pool with graceful shutdown in Go: drain in-flight work, reject new work, and wait for all goroutines to exit.
output:
<think>
Graceful shutdown requires three things:
1. Stop accepting new work (close the submit path or check a flag).
2. Allow in-flight work to complete.
3. Signal to the caller that shutdown is done.

The cleanest approach: a Pool struct with a jobs channel, a context for cancellation, and a WaitGroup. On Shutdown(), cancel the context and close the channel. Workers drain remaining buffered jobs before exiting.
</think>

```go
package main

import (
	"context"
	"errors"
	"fmt"
	"sync"
	"time"
)

var ErrShutdown = errors.New("pool is shut down")

type Pool struct {
	jobs   chan func()
	wg     sync.WaitGroup
	cancel context.CancelFunc
	ctx    context.Context
	once   sync.Once
}

func NewPool(workers, queueSize int) *Pool {
	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
	p := &Pool{
		jobs:   make(chan func(), queueSize),
		cancel: cancel,
		ctx:    ctx,
	}
	for i := 0; i < workers; i++ {
		p.wg.Add(1)
		go p.run()
	}
	return p
}

func (p *Pool) run() {
	defer p.wg.Done()
	for job := range p.jobs {
		job() // run job to completion; no cancellation inside job itself here
	}
}

// Submit enqueues a job. Returns ErrShutdown if pool is draining.
func (p *Pool) Submit(job func()) error {
	select {
	case <-p.ctx.Done():
		return ErrShutdown
	case p.jobs <- job:
		return nil
	}
}

// Shutdown stops new submissions, drains the queue, and waits for workers.
func (p *Pool) Shutdown() {
	p.once.Do(func() {
		p.cancel()    // reject new submits
		close(p.jobs) // signal workers to exit after draining
		p.wg.Wait()  // block until all workers are done
	})
}

func main() {
	pool := NewPool(3, 10)

	for i := 0; i < 8; i++ {
		i := i
		_ = pool.Submit(func() {
			time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)
			fmt.Println("job", i, "done")
		})
	}

	// Begin shutdown — already-submitted jobs complete.
	pool.Shutdown()
	fmt.Println("all workers exited")

	// New submits fail.
	if err := pool.Submit(func() {}); err != nil {
		fmt.Println("submit after shutdown:", err)
	}
}
```

`sync.Once` in `Shutdown` prevents double-close panics if multiple goroutines call Shutdown concurrently. Workers range over `p.jobs`, so they drain the buffered queue naturally after `close(p.jobs)`.

instruction: Implement an errgroup-based concurrent file processor that reads multiple files in parallel and returns a combined error if any file fails.
Demonstrate worker lifecycle management in Go: start, pause, resume, and stop a pool of workers using channels and sync primitives.
Use context.WithCancel to build a cancellable goroutine tree where cancelling the root propagates to all descendants.
Demonstrate context.WithTimeout for wrapping an HTTP call with a deadline, including proper cleanup and error checking.
output:
<think>
The key pitfalls:
1. Always defer cancel() even for WithTimeout — the context may be cancelled before the timeout fires and we must release resources.
2. Check `errors.Is(err, context.DeadlineExceeded)` not string comparison.
3. resp.Body.Close() must happen even when err == nil.
</think>

```go
package main

import (
	"context"
	"errors"
	"fmt"
	"io"
	"net/http"
	"time"
)

// fetchWithTimeout performs a GET with a strict deadline.
// Returns (body, nil) on success, or a wrapped error.
func fetchWithTimeout(url string, timeout time.Duration) (string, error) {
	// Always call cancel to release timer resources, even if timeout fires.
	ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), timeout)
	defer cancel()

	req, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, http.MethodGet, url, nil)
	if err != nil {
		return "", fmt.Errorf("build request: %w", err)
	}

	resp, err := http.DefaultClient.Do(req)
	if err != nil {
		// Distinguish timeout from network error.
		if errors.Is(ctx.Err(), context.DeadlineExceeded) {
			return "", fmt.Errorf("request timed out after %v", timeout)
		}
		return "", fmt.Errorf("do request: %w", err)
	}
	defer resp.Body.Close()

	if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
		return "", fmt.Errorf("unexpected status %d", resp.StatusCode)
	}

	body, err := io.ReadAll(resp.Body)
	if err != nil {
		return "", fmt.Errorf("read body: %w", err)
	}
	return string(body), nil
}

func main() {
	// Should succeed.
	body, err := fetchWithTimeout("https://httpbin.org/get", 5*time.Second)
	if err != nil {
		fmt.Println("error:", err)
	} else {
		fmt.Println("got", len(body), "bytes")
	}

	// Should time out.
	_, err = fetchWithTimeout("https://httpbin.org/delay/10", 500*time.Millisecond)
	if err != nil {
		fmt.Println("expected error:", err)
	}
}
```

Use `http.NewRequestWithContext` (not the deprecated `req.WithContext`) so the context is set at construction time. The client respects ctx cancellation mid-connection, mid-read, and mid-TLS handshake.

instruction: Demonstrate context.WithValue for passing request-scoped data (request ID, user) through a middleware chain in an HTTP server.
Show how to propagate context cancellation through a multi-level call chain including a database query simulation.
Implement a context-aware middleware that enforces per-request timeouts in an HTTP server and handles panics gracefully.
Implement context-aware I/O: read from an io.Reader with a context deadline, aborting the read if the context is cancelled.
Detect and fix a race condition in Go involving a shared counter accessed by multiple goroutines, using both mutex and atomic approaches.
output:
<think>
The classic race: multiple goroutines read-modify-write a non-atomic integer. The Go race detector (`-race`) catches this at runtime. Fix options: sync.Mutex (general purpose) or sync/atomic (integers only, lower overhead). Show both.
</think>

```go
package main

import (
	"fmt"
	"sync"
	"sync/atomic"
)

// BROKEN: race condition — do not use in production.
func brokenCounter(n int) int {
	count := 0
	var wg sync.WaitGroup
	for i := 0; i < n; i++ {
		wg.Add(1)
		go func() {
			defer wg.Done()
			count++ // DATA RACE: read-modify-write not atomic
		}()
	}
	wg.Wait()
	return count // may be less than n
}

// FIXED: mutex approach — correct for complex shared state.
func mutexCounter(n int) int {
	var mu sync.Mutex
	count := 0
	var wg sync.WaitGroup
	for i := 0; i < n; i++ {
		wg.Add(1)
		go func() {
			defer wg.Done()
			mu.Lock()
			count++
			mu.Unlock()
		}()
	}
	wg.Wait()
	return count
}

// FIXED: atomic approach — best for simple integer counters.
func atomicCounter(n int) int64 {
	var count int64
	var wg sync.WaitGroup
	for i := 0; i < n; i++ {
		wg.Add(1)
		go func() {
			defer wg.Done()
			atomic.AddInt64(&count, 1)
		}()
	}
	wg.Wait()
	return atomic.LoadInt64(&count)
}

func main() {
	const n = 10000
	// brokenCounter may print < 10000 and triggers -race detector.
	fmt.Println("mutex:", mutexCounter(n))   // always 10000
	fmt.Println("atomic:", atomicCounter(n)) // always 10000
}
```

Performance guidance:
- `atomic.AddInt64` has no lock overhead; use it for counters, flags, and version numbers.
- `sync.Mutex` is required when the critical section involves multiple fields that must be consistent together.
- Run `go test -race ./...` in CI — it adds ~10x overhead but catches races that never manifest in normal runs.

instruction: Compare sync.Map versus a mutex-protected map in Go: when to use each, with benchmarkable examples.
Demonstrate sync.Once for lazy singleton initialization and explain why double-checked locking is broken in Go without it.
Implement sync.Pool correctly to reuse allocated byte buffers across goroutines, and explain the common misuse patterns.
output:
<think>
The key insight about sync.Pool: objects can be evicted by the GC at any time between a Put and a Get. You must never store state in pooled objects that you rely on persisting. Always reset the object before returning it to the pool.
</think>

```go
package main

import (
	"bytes"
	"fmt"
	"sync"
)

// bufPool holds *bytes.Buffer objects.
// The New function is called when Get finds no cached object.
var bufPool = sync.Pool{
	New: func() any {
		// Allocate with an initial capacity to avoid early reallocations.
		return bytes.NewBuffer(make([]byte, 0, 256))
	},
}

// getBuffer retrieves a buffer from the pool, guaranteed to be empty.
func getBuffer() *bytes.Buffer {
	buf := bufPool.Get().(*bytes.Buffer)
	buf.Reset() // CRITICAL: always reset before use
	return buf
}

// putBuffer returns a buffer to the pool.
// Do NOT use the buffer after calling this.
func putBuffer(buf *bytes.Buffer) {
	// Avoid retaining large allocations in the pool.
	if buf.Cap() > 64*1024 { // 64 KB cap
		return // let GC collect it instead
	}
	bufPool.Put(buf)
}

func buildMessage(parts []string) string {
	buf := getBuffer()
	defer putBuffer(buf)

	for i, p := range parts {
		if i > 0 {
			buf.WriteByte(' ')
		}
		buf.WriteString(p)
	}
	return buf.String() // copy out before returning buf to pool
}

func main() {
	var wg sync.WaitGroup
	for i := 0; i < 5; i++ {
		i := i
		wg.Add(1)
		go func() {
			defer wg.Done()
			msg := buildMessage([]string{"goroutine", fmt.Sprint(i), "done"})
			fmt.Println(msg)
		}()
	}
	wg.Wait()
}
```

Common misuses:
1. **Returning a pooled object and continuing to use it** — causes data corruption when another goroutine gets it from the pool.
2. **Storing pointers to pooled objects** — same issue as above.
3. **Forgetting `Reset()`** — pooled buffers contain stale data from the previous user.
4. **Using Pool for connections or locks** — Pool is for temporary allocations only; use custom free-lists for resources with lifecycle.
5. **Retaining oversized buffers** — a 100MB buffer returned to the pool wastes memory; cap and discard.

instruction: Implement a thread-safe LRU cache in Go using sync.RWMutex and a doubly-linked list.
Demonstrate WaitGroup patterns in Go: the common leak/panic pitfall of calling Add inside goroutines, and how to fix it.
Implement goroutine leak detection and prevention in Go using runtime.NumGoroutine and goleak patterns.
output:
<think>
Goroutine leaks happen when: a goroutine is blocked on a channel with no sender/receiver, blocked on a mutex that will never unlock, or blocked in an infinite loop with no exit condition. Prevention: always pair goroutine launch with an exit condition (context, done channel, or closed input). Detection: compare goroutine count before/after.
</think>

```go
package main

import (
	"context"
	"fmt"
	"runtime"
	"time"
)

// LEAKY: goroutine blocks forever on ch if nobody sends.
func leakyFunction() {
	ch := make(chan int)
	go func() {
		v := <-ch // blocks forever if ch is never sent to or closed
		fmt.Println(v)
	}()
	// ch goes out of scope; goroutine is stuck forever.
}

// FIXED: always provide an exit via context or close.
func fixedFunction(ctx context.Context) {
	ch := make(chan int)
	go func() {
		select {
		case v := <-ch:
			fmt.Println(v)
		case <-ctx.Done():
			fmt.Println("goroutine: context cancelled, exiting")
		}
	}()
	// Caller controls lifetime via ctx.
}

// goroutineCount is a test helper to snapshot goroutine count.
func goroutineCount() int { return runtime.NumGoroutine() }

// assertNoLeak panics if goroutines leaked after fn completes.
func assertNoLeak(fn func()) {
	before := goroutineCount()
	fn()
	// Give goroutines a moment to finish.
	time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)
	runtime.GC() // force GC to collect finalizers
	after := goroutineCount()
	if after > before {
		panic(fmt.Sprintf("goroutine leak: %d before, %d after (+%d)", before, after, after-before))
	}
}

func main() {
	// Demonstrate leak detection.
	before := goroutineCount()
	leakyFunction()
	time.Sleep(50 * time.Millisecond)
	after := goroutineCount()
	fmt.Printf("after leaky: %d goroutines (leaked %d)\n", after, after-before)

	// Demonstrate clean function.
	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
	assertNoLeak(func() {
		fixedFunction(ctx)
		cancel() // signal goroutine to exit
		time.Sleep(50 * time.Millisecond)
	})
	fmt.Println("no leak detected")
}
```

For tests, use `go.uber.org/goleak` which is the production standard: `defer goleak.VerifyNone(t)` at the top of each test. It checks goroutine counts and prints stack traces of leaked goroutines. Always run tests with `-race` and goleak together.

instruction: Use atomic operations in Go for a lock-free stack implementation using compare-and-swap.
Write a table-driven test in Go for a string parsing function, including edge cases, parallel subtests, and proper test naming.
Build a mock HTTP client in Go using interfaces to enable unit testing of a function that calls an external API.
output:
<think>
The key: define an interface for the HTTP client behavior you need, not the concrete *http.Client. Your production code takes the interface; tests inject a fake.
</think>

```go
package main

import (
	"encoding/json"
	"errors"
	"io"
	"net/http"
	"strings"
	"testing"
)

// HTTPClient is the interface our code depends on — only the methods we use.
type HTTPClient interface {
	Do(req *http.Request) (*http.Response, error)
}

// WeatherService fetches weather data from an API.
type WeatherService struct {
	client  HTTPClient
	baseURL string
}

type WeatherResponse struct {
	Temp float64 `json:"temp"`
	City string  `json:"city"`
}

func (w *WeatherService) GetWeather(city string) (*WeatherResponse, error) {
	req, _ := http.NewRequest(http.MethodGet, w.baseURL+"/weather?city="+city, nil)
	resp, err := w.client.Do(req)
	if err != nil {
		return nil, err
	}
	defer resp.Body.Close()
	if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
		return nil, errors.New("unexpected status")
	}
	var result WeatherResponse
	if err := json.NewDecoder(resp.Body).Decode(&result); err != nil {
		return nil, err
	}
	return &result, nil
}

// mockHTTPClient is a test double for HTTPClient.
type mockHTTPClient struct {
	response *http.Response
	err      error
}

func (m *mockHTTPClient) Do(_ *http.Request) (*http.Response, error) {
	return m.response, m.err
}

func makeResponse(status int, body string) *http.Response {
	return &http.Response{
		StatusCode: status,
		Body:       io.NopCloser(strings.NewReader(body)),
	}
}

func TestGetWeather(t *testing.T) {
	cases := []struct {
		name     string
		mock     *mockHTTPClient
		city     string
		wantTemp float64
		wantErr  bool
	}{
		{
			name:     "success",
			mock:     &mockHTTPClient{response: makeResponse(200, `{"temp":22.5,"city":"London"}`)},
			city:     "London",
			wantTemp: 22.5,
		},
		{
			name:    "server error",
			mock:    &mockHTTPClient{response: makeResponse(500, "")},
			city:    "Paris",
			wantErr: true,
		},
		{
			name:    "network error",
			mock:    &mockHTTPClient{err: errors.New("connection refused")},
			city:    "Berlin",
			wantErr: true,
		},
	}

	for _, tc := range cases {
		tc := tc
		t.Run(tc.name, func(t *testing.T) {
			svc := &WeatherService{client: tc.mock, baseURL: "http://api.example.com"}
			result, err := svc.GetWeather(tc.city)
			if tc.wantErr {
				if err == nil {
					t.Error("expected error, got nil")
				}
				return
			}
			if err != nil {
				t.Fatal(err)
			}
			if result.Temp != tc.wantTemp {
				t.Errorf("got temp %v, want %v", result.Temp, tc.wantTemp)
			}
		})
	}
}
```

instruction: Use net/http/httptest to write an integration test for an HTTP handler in Go, testing status codes, headers, and response bodies.
Demonstrate interface composition in Go: build a ReadWriteSeeker from smaller interfaces and implement a type that satisfies the composed interface.
Implement the functional options pattern in Go for a configurable server, demonstrating how to handle defaults, validation, and optional fields cleanly.
output:
<think>
Functional options (Rob Pike pattern) solve the problem of structs with many optional fields. Instead of a Config struct passed by value (which forces all fields to be exported and callers to know defaults), each option is a function that mutates an internal config. This lets you add new options without breaking callers.
</think>

```go
package main

import (
	"errors"
	"fmt"
	"time"
)

// serverConfig holds all options — unexported, controlled via Option functions.
type serverConfig struct {
	host           string
	port           int
	readTimeout    time.Duration
	writeTimeout   time.Duration
	maxConnections int
	tlsEnabled     bool
	certFile       string
	keyFile        string
}

// defaults returns a config with sensible defaults.
func defaults() *serverConfig {
	return &serverConfig{
		host:           "0.0.0.0",
		port:           8080,
		readTimeout:    30 * time.Second,
		writeTimeout:   30 * time.Second,
		maxConnections: 1000,
	}
}

// Option is a function that modifies serverConfig.
type Option func(*serverConfig) error

// WithHost sets the bind address.
func WithHost(host string) Option {
	return func(c *serverConfig) error {
		if host == "" {
			return errors.New("host cannot be empty")
		}
		c.host = host
		return nil
	}
}

// WithPort sets the TCP port.
func WithPort(port int) Option {
	return func(c *serverConfig) error {
		if port < 1 || port > 65535 {
			return fmt.Errorf("invalid port %d", port)
		}
		c.port = port
		return nil
	}
}

// WithTimeouts sets read and write timeouts.
func WithTimeouts(read, write time.Duration) Option {
	return func(c *serverConfig) error {
		if read <= 0 || write <= 0 {
			return errors.New("timeouts must be positive")
		}
		c.readTimeout = read
		c.writeTimeout = write
		return nil
	}
}

// WithTLS enables TLS with the given certificate and key files.
func WithTLS(certFile, keyFile string) Option {
	return func(c *serverConfig) error {
		if certFile == "" || keyFile == "" {
			return errors.New("cert and key files required for TLS")
		}
		c.tlsEnabled = true
		c.certFile = certFile
		c.keyFile = keyFile
		return nil
	}
}

// Server is the public type.
type Server struct {
	cfg *serverConfig
}

// NewServer constructs a Server, applying options over defaults.
func NewServer(opts ...Option) (*Server, error) {
	cfg := defaults()
	for _, opt := range opts {
		if err := opt(cfg); err != nil {
			return nil, fmt.Errorf("server option: %w", err)
		}
	}
	return &Server{cfg: cfg}, nil
}

func (s *Server) Addr() string {
	proto := "http"
	if s.cfg.tlsEnabled {
		proto = "https"
	}
	return fmt.Sprintf("%s://%s:%d", proto, s.cfg.host, s.cfg.port)
}

func main() {
	// Minimal: use all defaults.
	s1, _ := NewServer()
	fmt.Println(s1.Addr())

	// Custom port and timeouts.
	s2, err := NewServer(
		WithPort(9090),
		WithTimeouts(10*time.Second, 10*time.Second),
	)
	if err != nil {
		fmt.Println("error:", err)
		return
	}
	fmt.Println(s2.Addr())

	// Invalid option returns error without partial construction.
	_, err = NewServer(WithPort(99999))
	fmt.Println("invalid port:", err)
}
```

Returning `error` from `Option` (rather than the simpler `func(*serverConfig)`) enables validation at construction time. The alternative — panicking inside options — is idiomatic for library-internal errors but unacceptable for user-supplied values.

Implement a concurrent map-reduce pipeline in Go that processes a slice of words counting frequency across multiple goroutines.
output:
<think>
Map phase: split input into chunks, each goroutine counts frequency in its chunk independently. Reduce phase: merge partial maps. The key insight is that the map phase is embarrassingly parallel — chunks don't share state. Reduction is sequential (or can be done in a tree) to produce the final map.
</think>

```go
package main

import (
	"fmt"
	"strings"
	"sync"
)

type FreqMap map[string]int

// mapPhase counts word frequency in a slice chunk.
func mapPhase(words []string) FreqMap {
	freq := make(FreqMap)
	for _, w := range words {
		freq[strings.ToLower(w)]++
	}
	return freq
}

// reducePhase merges partial frequency maps.
func reducePhase(partial []FreqMap) FreqMap {
	result := make(FreqMap)
	for _, m := range partial {
		for k, v := range m {
			result[k] += v
		}
	}
	return result
}

// WordFrequency counts word frequency using a concurrent map-reduce.
func WordFrequency(words []string, workers int) FreqMap {
	if workers <= 0 || len(words) == 0 {
		return mapPhase(words)
	}

	chunkSize := (len(words) + workers - 1) / workers

	partials := make([]FreqMap, workers)
	var wg sync.WaitGroup

	for i := 0; i < workers; i++ {
		start := i * chunkSize
		if start >= len(words) {
			break
		}
		end := start + chunkSize
		if end > len(words) {
			end = len(words)
		}

		wg.Add(1)
		go func(idx int, chunk []string) {
			defer wg.Done()
			partials[idx] = mapPhase(chunk)
		}(i, words[start:end])
	}

	wg.Wait()

	// Filter out nil partials (fewer words than workers).
	var active []FreqMap
	for _, p := range partials {
		if p != nil {
			active = append(active, p)
		}
	}

	return reducePhase(active)
}

func main() {
	text := "the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog the fox"
	words := strings.Fields(text)

	freq := WordFrequency(words, 4)

	// Print top words.
	for word, count := range freq {
		fmt.Printf("%s: %d\n", word, count)
	}
}
```

Writing to `partials[idx]` is safe without a mutex because each goroutine writes to a distinct slice index — this is the same guarantee that makes parallel sorting safe. The reduce phase is sequential, which is correct: reduction is typically cheap compared to the map phase.

instruction: Implement a singleflight-style deduplication pattern in Go that coalesces concurrent duplicate requests into one.
Implement context propagation through goroutines using context.Value with request-scoped tracing IDs.
Show how to implement a retry pattern with exponential backoff and jitter in Go, using context for cancellation.
output:
<think>
Exponential backoff: wait = base * 2^attempt. Jitter: add random ± fraction to avoid thundering herd when many clients retry simultaneously. Max cap on wait to prevent unbounded delay. Context cancellation exits early.
</think>

```go
package main

import (
	"context"
	"errors"
	"fmt"
	"math/rand"
	"time"
)

// RetryConfig controls backoff behaviour.
type RetryConfig struct {
	MaxAttempts int
	BaseDelay   time.Duration
	MaxDelay    time.Duration
	Jitter      float64 // fraction of delay to add randomly [0, 1]
}

var DefaultRetryConfig = RetryConfig{
	MaxAttempts: 5,
	BaseDelay:   100 * time.Millisecond,
	MaxDelay:    30 * time.Second,
	Jitter:      0.2,
}

// Retry calls fn up to cfg.MaxAttempts times with exponential backoff.
// If fn returns nil, retrying stops and nil is returned.
// If ctx is cancelled, returns ctx.Err().
func Retry(ctx context.Context, cfg RetryConfig, fn func(ctx context.Context) error) error {
	var lastErr error
	for attempt := 0; attempt < cfg.MaxAttempts; attempt++ {
		lastErr = fn(ctx)
		if lastErr == nil {
			return nil
		}
		// Check if the error is permanent (should not retry).
		var permErr *PermanentError
		if errors.As(lastErr, &permErr) {
			return lastErr
		}
		if attempt == cfg.MaxAttempts-1 {
			break
		}
		// Exponential backoff with jitter.
		delay := cfg.BaseDelay * (1 << attempt)
		if delay > cfg.MaxDelay {
			delay = cfg.MaxDelay
		}
		jitter := time.Duration(float64(delay) * cfg.Jitter * (rand.Float64()*2 - 1))
		delay += jitter
		if delay < 0 {
			delay = 0
		}

		fmt.Printf("attempt %d failed: %v — retrying in %v\n", attempt+1, lastErr, delay.Round(time.Millisecond))
		select {
		case <-time.After(delay):
		case <-ctx.Done():
			return ctx.Err()
		}
	}
	return fmt.Errorf("all %d attempts failed: %w", cfg.MaxAttempts, lastErr)
}

// PermanentError marks an error as non-retryable.
type PermanentError struct{ Err error }

func (e *PermanentError) Error() string { return "permanent: " + e.Err.Error() }
func (e *PermanentError) Unwrap() error { return e.Err }
func Permanent(err error) error        { return &PermanentError{Err: err} }

func main() {
	attempts := 0
	callFn := func(ctx context.Context) error {
		attempts++
		if attempts < 4 {
			return errors.New("transient error")
		}
		return nil
	}

	ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 5*time.Second)
	defer cancel()

	err := Retry(ctx, DefaultRetryConfig, callFn)
	if err != nil {
		fmt.Println("failed:", err)
	} else {
		fmt.Printf("succeeded on attempt %d\n", attempts)
	}
}
```

Implement a connection pool in Go with acquire/release semantics, idle timeout, and health checking.
output:
<think>
A connection pool needs: a free list of connections, a way to block callers when pool is empty, health check before reuse, idle timeout to evict stale connections, and a cap on total connections.

Use a buffered channel as the free list — it naturally blocks on empty (acquire waits) and blocks on full (release waits if misused). Track total count with an atomic to limit creation.
</think>

```go
package main

import (
	"context"
	"errors"
	"fmt"
	"sync/atomic"
	"time"
)

type Conn struct {
	ID        int
	LastUsed  time.Time
	Healthy   bool
}

func (c *Conn) IsHealthy() bool {
	// Simulate health check.
	return c.Healthy && time.Since(c.LastUsed) < 30*time.Second
}

type Pool struct {
	free    chan *Conn
	total   atomic.Int64
	maxSize int64
	newConn func() (*Conn, error)
}

func NewPool(size int, newConn func() (*Conn, error)) *Pool {
	return &Pool{
		free:    make(chan *Conn, size),
		maxSize: int64(size),
		newConn: newConn,
	}
}

// Acquire returns a healthy connection or blocks until one is available.
func (p *Pool) Acquire(ctx context.Context) (*Conn, error) {
	for {
		// Try to get a free connection first.
		select {
		case conn := <-p.free:
			if conn.IsHealthy() {
				return conn, nil
			}
			// Unhealthy; discard and try to create a new one.
			p.total.Add(-1)
		default:
		}

		// Create new connection if under cap.
		if p.total.Add(1) <= p.maxSize {
			conn, err := p.newConn()
			if err != nil {
				p.total.Add(-1)
				return nil, fmt.Errorf("create connection: %w", err)
			}
			return conn, nil
		}
		p.total.Add(-1) // rolled back the increment

		// Pool full — wait for a release.
		select {
		case conn := <-p.free:
			if conn.IsHealthy() {
				return conn, nil
			}
			p.total.Add(-1) // discard unhealthy
		case <-ctx.Done():
			return nil, ctx.Err()
		}
	}
}

// Release returns a connection to the pool.
func (p *Pool) Release(conn *Conn) {
	conn.LastUsed = time.Now()
	select {
	case p.free <- conn:
	default:
		// Pool is full (shouldn't happen with correct usage); discard.
		p.total.Add(-1)
	}
}

var connID atomic.Int64

func main() {
	pool := NewPool(3, func() (*Conn, error) {
		id := int(connID.Add(1))
		fmt.Println("creating connection", id)
		return &Conn{ID: id, Healthy: true, LastUsed: time.Now()}, nil
	})

	ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 5*time.Second)
	defer cancel()

	conn1, _ := pool.Acquire(ctx)
	conn2, _ := pool.Acquire(ctx)
	conn3, _ := pool.Acquire(ctx)

	fmt.Printf("acquired: %d %d %d\n", conn1.ID, conn2.ID, conn3.ID)

	pool.Release(conn1)
	pool.Release(conn2)

	// Should reuse released connections.
	conn4, _ := pool.Acquire(ctx)
	fmt.Println("reused connection:", conn4.ID) // 1 or 2

	pool.Release(conn3)
	pool.Release(conn4)

	// Simulate unhealthy connection.
	conn3.Healthy = false
	pool.Release(conn3)
	conn5, _ := pool.Acquire(ctx)
	fmt.Println("new conn after unhealthy discard:", conn5.ID)
}
```
